SUMMARY:
AI in Pricing Artificial Intelligence (AI) transforms business pricing strategies by providing game-changing insights that boost profitability and competitiveness. This paper examines how pricing leaders can improve margin optimization, adjust to shifting market conditions, and optimize pricing strategies by incorporating AI into their daily operations. Learn about the most recent advancements, practical applications, and future possibilities of AI in B2B pricing to help your company stay ahead of the curve. Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming a more potent instrument that helps companies to increase productivity, optimize profitability, and make data-driven pricing decisions. By using cutting-edge algorithms and machine learning models to evaluate enormous volumes of data, spot trends, and suggest the best prices, artificial intelligence (AI) is completely reinventing pricing methods. Using AI to Optimize Your Pricing Strategy: AI in Pricing

Leveraging Artificial Intelligence in Pricing
=============================================

*    [PPS](https://www.pricingsociety.com/members-area/ea3306e6-e8fb-4b11-adb8-120bc5c3bfff/profile)
*   Oct 30, 2024
*   5 min read

Updated: Feb 13

![Image 2: Kiran Gange blog image](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_aa20a0cdf9614c98bdc99580ac19361a~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_26,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_aa20a0cdf9614c98bdc99580ac19361a~mv2.png)

Author: [Kiran Gange](https://www.linkedin.com/in/kirangange/)

Artificial Intelligence (AI) transforms business pricing strategies by providing game-changing insights that boost profitability and competitiveness. This paper examines how pricing leaders can improve margin optimization, adjust to shifting market conditions, and optimize pricing strategies by incorporating AI into their daily operations. Learn about the most recent advancements, practical applications, and future possibilities of AI in B2B pricing to help your company stay ahead of the curve.

![Image 3: pricing graph](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_631e4522f222442f9c01877f17f47462~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_26,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_631e4522f222442f9c01877f17f47462~mv2.png)

**Figure 1:**Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Retail Market Size, 2022 - 2032 (USD Billion)

**Introduction:**

In the current data-driven and fast-paced market climate, companies are under growing pressure to optimize their pricing strategies. Conventional pricing strategies, which frequently depend on human analysis and historical data, are insufficient to stay competitive. Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming a more potent instrument that helps companies to increase productivity, optimize profitability, and make data-driven pricing decisions. By using cutting-edge algorithms and machine learning models to evaluate enormous volumes of data, spot trends, and suggest the best prices, artificial intelligence (AI) is completely reinventing pricing methods.

● Pricing algorithms may boost revenue from 1% to 5% and lengthen the customer life cycle by 20%, according to a comprehensive worldwide assessment carried out by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and the BCG Henderson Institute (BHI).

● By 2028, it's expected that the retail industry will have invested $24.1 billion in AI-based solutions.

●Personalized suggestions, content creation, and predictive analytics are projected to be the top applications of generative AI by global marketers and advertisers in 2023.

1.   **Analytics and AI for Margin Optimization**:

AI is essential for improving margin optimization because it gives pricing teams instant access to information about cost structures, market dynamics, and consumer behavior. AI is able to find chances to modify prices in a way that maximizes profit margins while maintaining market share by examining these aspects. AI, for example, can identify underperforming product lines and recommend price adjustments to match competition pricing and market demand. By using a flexible approach, companies may protect their profitability while retaining a competitive advantage.

**Example:**A multinational electronics firm regularly tracked market trends and competition activity using pricing analytics driven by AI. The AI system saw a trend where rivals regularly changed their rates during the busiest times of year for sales. The manufacturer increased its market share and increased its profits by 15% by aggressively adjusting its pricing approach.

![Image 4: pricing graph 2](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_a114d5c2af10458cac384be1e30e5239~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_24,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_a114d5c2af10458cac384be1e30e5239~mv2.png)

**Figure 2:**Benefits of Artificial Intelligence (AI) for Retail Business Worldwide, 2022

1.   **Leveraging AI for Everyday Pricing Operations and New Product Pricing**:

AI can be used to expedite daily pricing processes in addition to long-term strategic decision-making. Routine operations like pricing monitoring, updating pricing models based on fresh data, and identifying anomalies that need attention can be automated by AI. Pricing teams may now concentrate on more strategic tasks, including introducing new goods or breaking into untapped markets, thanks to this automation.

AI can forecast the ideal launch price for new products by examining past sales data, consumer preferences, and industry trends. Businesses can make well-informed decisions that strike a balance between profitability and market acceptance by modeling several price situations.

**Example:**A software-as-a-service (SaaS) provider used AI to optimize subscription service pricing. Artificial Intelligence suggested tiered pricing structures based on the needs of various client categories by examining customer usage habits. This strategy led to 10% more subscription renewals and higher customer satisfaction.

#### **2.Use Cases and Best Practices in AI-Powered Pricing from Around the World:**

Global adoption of AI in a variety of industries demonstrates its adaptability and potency as a pricing strategy. These are a few noteworthy use cases:

![Image 5: chart 3](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_a14b3d50895e4255949692263bd62a73~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_13,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_a14b3d50895e4255949692263bd62a73~mv2.png)

**Figure 3:**AI Use Cases in Consumer Goods and Retail Industry Worldwide, 2020

**Retail:**To improve markdown pricing, a top fashion retailer analyzed real-time sales data using artificial intelligence. The retailer raised sales during end-of-season discounts and eliminated surplus inventory by 20% by employing AI-driven markdown methods.

**Manufacturing:**Using AI, a European supplier of auto components dynamically modified prices based on the cost of raw materials and the prices of rival companies. Despite changes in input costs, the company was nevertheless profitable thanks to its flexible pricing strategy.

**E-commerce:**An Asian online marketplace used artificial intelligence (AI) to track rival prices and modify its own pricing policy. Artificial intelligence algorithms were able to identify price reductions made by rivals and automatically modify the company's prices to maintain competitiveness while safeguarding profit margins.

**Example:**Sephora's Beauty Insider program uses AI-powered chatbots to offer personalized beauty consultations through its "Beauty IQ" tool. This virtual assistant analyzes customer preferences and skin concerns, recommending customized product regimens. The program boasts a 25% increase in customer engagement and a 10% rise in average order value.

![Image 6: sephora graphic](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_6099dfa75a9c492ab3fc2106f484d29c~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_31,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_6099dfa75a9c492ab3fc2106f484d29c~mv2.png)

**Figure 4:**Beauty IQ tool by Sephora _(Source: Sephora)_

1.   **Future Potential of AI in Pricing:**

Artificial intelligence in pricing has a bright future ahead of it. Artificial intelligence (AI) systems will become ever more proficient at comprehending intricate market dynamics and customer behavior as machine learning and natural language processing technologies progress. Real-time sentiment analysis will probably be a feature of future AI-powered pricing solutions, enabling companies to predict how customers will respond to price adjustments and modify their plans accordingly.

AI can also improve collaborative pricing models, in which retailers and suppliers collaborate to optimize pricing across the supply chain. By using a comprehensive strategy, we can increase productivity, cut expenses, and provide outcomes that benefit all parties involved.

1.   **Using AI to Optimize Your Pricing Strategy:**

Businesses should adhere to these best practices in order to successfully use AI in pricing:

**Invest in High-Quality Data:**AI needs data to function. Having complete, current, and correct data is essential for AI to produce insightful results.

**Start Small and Scale:**To gauge the effect of AI on particular product lines or market niches, start with trial initiatives. Based on demonstrated outcomes, gradually scale AI integration throughout the whole pricing strategy.

**Continuous Learning:**In order for AI models to adjust to shifting market conditions, they require frequent updates and recalibration. Ongoing education guarantees AI's continued efficacy in providing ideal price advice.

**Work Together with Experts:**Using pricing software platforms and collaborating with AI experts can help you get the knowledge and resources you need to successfully apply AI-driven pricing strategies.

![Image 7: detailed chart](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/ca924f_b8d184a8729c4636bda39cb601984cd8~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_49,h_24,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/ca924f_b8d184a8729c4636bda39cb601984cd8~mv2.png)

**Figure 5:**What Advanced Pricing Methods Improve

**Conclusion:**

With its ability to facilitate data-driven decision-making, optimize margins, and improve competitive positioning, artificial intelligence is revolutionizing the world of business-to-business pricing. Businesses can better navigate the intricacies of today's market dynamics and enhance their pricing strategies by utilizing AI. AI technology will play a more and more important role in pricing as it develops, making it a vital tool for companies looking to achieve both sustainable development and excellent pricing. Businesses that adopt AI-driven pricing strategies now will be better positioned for success in the cutthroat marketplaces of the future.

# How AI Is Transforming Pricing Management: A Comprehensive Guide

By[Jyothish R](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/jyothishr/ "https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/people/jyothishr/"),

Forbes Councils Member.

for 
[Forbes Technology Council](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/)

COUNCIL POST

Expertise from Forbes Councils members, operated under license. Opinions expressed are those of the author.

 | Membership (fee-based)

May 13, 2025, 08:15am EDT

*Jyothish, CTO & Global Delivery Officer at* [*AIMLEAP*](https://aimleap.com/)*, is an expert in data engineering and IT services with 14+ years of leadership experience*

As price pressure increases, [68%](https://www.simon-kucher.com/sites/default/files/media-document/2024-01/sk_state_of_pricing_2024_gps_report_summary.pdf) of companies plan to raise prices to keep up with inflation. In light of this, with AI-based software, companies can dynamically optimize prices in real time and potentially achieve greater profitability and competitiveness.

In this article, I'll look at how AI is transforming the science of price management and pushing pricing managers to keep up with the times. This is a topic I've been involved in for over 14 years as an expert in data and IT solutions.

PROMOTED

## The Challenges Of Traditional Pricing Management

Determining prices in a business environment involves factors beyond number selection. Organizations need to monitor market transformations, evolving customer demands and their competitors' actions. Traditional pricing methods perform poorly because they require long timeframes for change implementation and work with old information while opportunity windows remain unexploited.

Key challenges surrounding traditional price management include:

• Static pricing approaches pose a problem because traditional systems do not work well with fluctuating market dynamics.

• Reacting through manual processes leads businesses to delayed pricing action and unrealized potential for enhanced optimization.

• Competitor analysis becomes difficult due to insufficient market data, which can result in reduced market competition abilities.

• Using rigid pricing models can result in lower customer engagement and reduced conversion levels.

These challenges highlight the need for more agile, data-driven approaches to AI pricing management.

## The Role Of AI In Pricing Management

AI transforms prices through automated operations and predictive statistical analysis. Live pricing decisions made through machine learning give businesses the ability to set accurate prices. The results of a recent survey indicate that [85%](https://www.pwc.be/en/news-publications/archive/artificial-intelligence-may-be-game-changer-for-pricing.html) of CEOs predict AI will alter their business operations and pricing methods within five years.

AI offers several key benefits for pricing management, including:

• AI can achieve data-driven pricing decisions by analyzing extensive datasets.

• The system can adjust product prices using AI-powered market data and customer actions.

• AI enables businesses to enhance their profitability by helping them optimize their prices for higher revenue and effectively compete in price wars.

• AI can help deliver a better customer experience by tailoring prices according to individual customers.

• Price scraping tools let businesses track competitors’ pricing strategies.

## AI-Powered Pricing Management Tools

AI-powered pricing tools are powerful for today’s pricing strategies. Key features of AI pricing tools include the following:

• AI uses predictive analysis to determine future market demands, which enables businesses to modify prices in a planned manner.

• Pricing algorithms can implement dynamic price changes because they use real-time market data.

• Automated tools gather insights from competitor pricing strategies.

• AI tools can create various pricing scenarios, which the system uses to determine optimal price points.

Business systems can benefit from these tools because they provide smooth connectivity with existing platforms, which enhances operational effectiveness. By adopting AI-driven pricing management tools, businesses can enhance pricing accuracy, improve response times and achieve significant revenue growth.

## AI Pricing Strategies

AI-based pricing strategies use machine learning to optimize decisions, ensuring businesses stay competitive and maximize revenue.

Some strategies include:

**• Dynamic Pricing:** AI can make real-time price adjustments while considering current demand levels, competitor market rates and individual consumer actions. Implementing this approach can preserve business competitiveness.

**• Personalized Pricing:** AI can produce personalized pricing by evaluating data such as customer browsing patterns and purchase records. This method can enhance conversion success and keep customers returning.

**• Value-Based Pricing:** Using AI to determine customer value perception enables companies to establish prices by evaluating market patterns and consumer input. This method can optimize prices for customer satisfaction and business profitability.

## Optimizing Pricing With AI

Implementing AI enables organizations to achieve pricing equilibrium between business profitability and market performance. Key techniques include:

• Machine learning models refine pricing strategies by learning from trends and customer behavior.

• AI conducts price elasticity research to monitor how price changes influence market demand, after which it performs automatic price modifications.

• Predicting optimal price points relies on regression analysis and neural networks that use historical data.

## Easy Steps To Implement AI In Pricing Management

To successfully integrate AI into pricing management, follow these steps:

1. Evaluate present price strategies to understand how AI can enhance their operations.

2. Select AI tools that fulfill organizational requirements and pricing targets.

3. Provide training to your team members regarding AI tool applications and pricing data interpretation methods.

4. Track user performance through precise KPIs that monitor the success of pricing models.

5. Monitor and refine strategies to continuously improve your AI pricing models based on performance and market insights.

## Challenges Of AI Adoption

The numerous advantages of AI systems come with potential difficulties, including privacy concerns about customer data, the absence of expert technical skills and the avoidance of adopting new approaches.

Here's how to address them:

• To protect customer data, compliance laws should be abided by. To do this, implement robust privacy protection standards.

• Organizations should offer training programs or recruit AI experts to develop internal AI capabilities.

• Information distribution about AI benefits helps stakeholders implement AI smoothly and can secure buy-in for these programs.

## AI Is Changing How We Approach Pricing

With AI, transforming your price management strategy is more achievable than ever. By focusing on real-time price adjustments, personalized offers and competitor analysis, those who implement AI solutions can better understand their market performance.

# The Ultimate Guide to Pricing Your AI Products: Strategies for Growth (Part 1)

### 

### Understanding the AI Product Pricing Landscape

Pricing your AI products isn’t as straightforward as traditional SaaS pricing. Factors such as computational costs, data dependencies, evolving regulatory frameworks, and customer perceptions of AI value all play a role in determining the optimal price point.

Before settling on a strategy, consider:

* **Value creation:** How does your AI product drive business outcomes?
* **Cost structure:** What are the major cost drivers (e.g., computing power, data acquisition, model training)?
* **Customer segmentation:** Who are your key customers, and how price-sensitive are they?
* **Competitive dynamics:** How do similar AI products price their solutions?
* **Scalability:** Will your pricing model sustain profitability as usage grows?

#### 

#### **AI Product Pricing by the Numbers**

The AI market is experiencing rapid growth, with projections indicating a compound annual growth rate of 37.3% from 2023 to 2030, ultimately reaching a value of $1.85 trillion, according to Grand View Research. Pricing for AI services varies widely, as seen with OpenAI’s ChatGPT Pro plan, which costs $20 per month for individual users, while enterprise clients pay significantly more for custom integrations. Google Cloud AI follows a usage-based pricing model, with costs ranging from $0.006 per prediction to $5 per thousand text classifications, highlighting the variability in AI product pricing structures.

Despite the expanding market, cost remains a major hurdle for businesses. A 2023 report from S&P Global found that more than half of AI decision-makers in top companies face financial barriers when implementing the latest AI tools. Additionally, research from IBM’s Institute for Business Value predicts that computing costs will rise by 89% between 2023 and 2025, with 70% of executives attributing this increase to the growing adoption of generative AI. 

 

### Key AI Product Pricing Models & Their Trade-Offs

Choosing the right pricing model is critical for AI companies looking to balance customer adoption, revenue growth, and profitability. Different pricing strategies come with their own advantages and challenges, affecting everything from customer retention to financial predictability. Below, we explore some of the most common AI product pricing models, their real-world applications, and the trade-offs businesses should consider when selecting the right approach.

#### **1. Usage-based Pricing (consumption-based Pricing)**

**Overview**  
In this model, customers pay based on how much they use the AI service, making it ideal for businesses that process large volumes of data or require AI-driven automation. Pricing could be based on API calls, computing resources, or AI model inferences.

Usage-based pricing is ideal for AI businesses where demand fluctuates, allowing customers to scale usage based on actual needs. This model works well for AI-powered APIs like OpenAI and Google AI, where pricing is based on API calls, enabling seamless integration into other applications. Cloud-based AI services such as AWS SageMaker also benefit from this model, as customers pay for computing power and storage on an as-needed basis. Additionally, predictive analytics platforms that rely on dynamic data processing—such as AI-driven business intelligence, fraud detection, and real-time decision-making—thrive under this model, as their value is closely tied to data volume and processing requirements.

**Real-Life Examples**

* **OpenAI’s GPT-4 API** charges $0.03 per 1,000 input tokens and $0.06 per 1,000 output tokens, ensuring that customers pay proportionally for their usage.
* **Amazon Bedrock (AWS AI Service)** charges per inference request and model usage, ensuring that businesses only pay for the computational power they use.
* **Google Cloud’s Vertex AI** bills based on the number of predictions, training hours, and storage used, making it cost-effective for AI developers.

**Pros & Cons**

Usage-based pricing lowers barriers to entry, making AI services accessible to startups and small teams without significant upfront costs. It aligns revenue with customer success, scaling spending as usage grows. The flexibility appeals to both enterprises and cost-conscious businesses, while transparent cost alignment helps customers budget more effectively.

However, cost unpredictability poses a major risk, with usage spikes leading to unexpected expenses that make budgeting difficult. Vendors must also manage high infrastructure costs to maintain profitability. Complex billing structures can confuse customers, increasing churn risk if pricing isn’t carefully designed. To succeed, AI providers must invest in clear documentation, usage tracking tools, and cost optimization strategies to ensure customers understand and control their spending.

#### **2. Seat-based pricing**

**Overview**

Customers pay a fixed fee per user, making pricing predictable and easy to scale within organizations. This is popular in B2B SaaS AI solutions.

Per-user pricing is ideal for AI-powered enterprise software, such as AI-enhanced ERP or CRM systems, where each user benefits individually from platform access. It also suits AI-driven collaboration tools, like document management or chatbots, where team-wide adoption increases engagement and value.

**Real-Life Examples**

* **Jasper AI**, a content-generation platform, offers per-seat pricing starting at $49 per user per month, making it accessible for teams.
* **Anthropic’s Claude AI** for Enterprise follows a per-user model, making it simple for businesses to onboard teams.
* **Notion AI** incorporates per-seat pricing in its business plans, where AI-powered content generation tools are available for each user at an additional monthly cost.

**Pros & Cons**

Per-user pricing offers simplicity and transparency, making it easy for businesses to predict expenses without the complexity of usage-based models. It scales naturally with organizational adoption, increasing revenue as companies onboard more employees. This model provides AI vendors with financial stability through recurring revenue while fostering deeper integration and collaboration, particularly for tools designed for team-based workflows. Additionally, opportunities for bundling and upselling premium features can drive higher revenue per user.

However, this model may not align with actual usage, leading low-usage customers to feel overcharged. High per-user costs can also limit widespread enterprise adoption, prompting businesses to restrict AI access to select employees. Companies with fluctuating head counts may find it too rigid, and customers often expect continuous improvements to justify recurring fees. There’s also a risk of underutilization, with businesses canceling unused seats or sharing logins to cut costs. To succeed, AI providers must ensure strong engagement and clearly communicate the ongoing value of their solution. 

#### **3. Tiered Pricing**

**Overview**  
Customers choose from different pricing tiers that offer varying levels of features, capacity, or performance. This provides flexibility while encouraging users to upgrade as their needs grow.

Tiered pricing is ideal for AI SaaS platforms catering to different user segments, such as individual users versus enterprises. It works well for AI-enhanced CRM tools, like AI-powered customer insights software, as well as generative AI applications, such as AI-powered video editing and design tools. These businesses benefit from the flexibility of tiered pricing, which allows them to serve a diverse range of customers while maintaining a sustainable revenue model.

**Real-Life Examples**

* **OpenAI:** ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) provides enhanced performance compared to the free version, while enterprise users get additional security, admin controls, and customization.
* **Figma AI:** Integrates AI-powered design features, offers tiered pricing where free users get limited AI-generated design elements, while paid tiers unlock more automation and collaboration tools.
* **Hugging Face:** offers free, pro ($9/month), and enterprise tiers, providing flexibility based on customer needs.

**Pros & Cons**

Tiered pricing offers flexible entry points, allowing customers to start at a lower cost or even for free, reducing the barrier to entry for price-sensitive users. This approach helps increase adoption, particularly for individual users, startups, and enterprises with varying budgets. As customers scale or require premium features, they are naturally incentivized to upgrade, driving customer lifetime value through a built-in growth path. The model captures a wide range of customers, catering to diverse needs across individuals, small businesses, and large enterprises. It also balances affordability with profitability, generating high-margin revenue from premium tiers. By allowing customers to grow at their own pace, tiered pricing encourages long-term retention, as users can downgrade instead of canceling entirely.

However, tiered pricing can be complex to manage. If lower tiers offer too much value, customers may see no reason to upgrade. AI companies must carefully optimize pricing and features to avoid overwhelming customers with choices and prevent feature bloat. Additionally, offering too many pricing options can cause decision paralysis, leading to potential abandonment of purchases. Customers in lower tiers may also feel dissatisfied if key features are locked behind higher-priced plans, which could lead to frustration and churn. If customers fail to upgrade, revenue growth may stagnate, requiring effective conversion strategies to encourage users to move to premium tiers.

#### **4. Success-based Pricing**

**Overview**  
Customers only pay when the AI solution delivers measurable success, such as generating leads, increasing conversions, or automating tasks effectively. This is common in AI solutions with clear performance metrics.  
Success-based pricing is best suited for AI-driven lead generation tools, such as sales automation AI, where businesses only pay for leads that convert. It also works well for marketing automation platforms that focus on improving conversion rates, ensuring that customers see a direct return on investment. Additionally, predictive analytics tools that optimize business outcomes—such as AI-driven pricing models—can benefit from this approach, as businesses are more willing to pay when they see measurable improvements in efficiency and profitability.  

**Real-Life Examples**

* **Drift AI:** Charges based on the number of qualified leads or closed deals.
* **Cresta AI:** AI-powered sales coaching platform, charges businesses based on the percentage increase in sales productivity driven by AI insights.
* **Intercom:** Intercom’s AI chatbot employs a success-based pricing model, charging customers only when a customer’s issue is resolved or they don’t return within 24 hours

**Pros & Cons**

Success-based pricing aligns costs with customer success, allowing businesses to pay only when they see tangible value from AI solutions. This reduces hesitation and the risk of upfront investment, making it particularly effective for performance-driven industries like sales, marketing, healthcare, and finance. By eliminating upfront costs, this model lowers financial risk and encourages adoption, especially in conservative sectors such as healthcare and finance that require proven results before committing. When AI solutions demonstrate real impact, customers are more willing to pay a premium, and the model creates a clear value proposition. Success-based pricing also fosters long-term partnerships, as AI vendors are incentivized to continually optimize performance.

However, success-based pricing can be challenging to implement and track, as measuring outcomes can be difficult, especially with intangible metrics like customer satisfaction or decision-making improvements. Clear and mutually agreed-upon success metrics are essential to avoid friction between the AI provider and the customer. The model can also lead to revenue unpredictability, as vendor earnings depend on customer success, which can vary based on market conditions or execution challenges. Additionally, the need to define success metrics can lengthen sales cycles, as enterprises may take months to negotiate terms and may require case studies or pilot programs to demonstrate ROI.

#### **5. Hybrid Pricing Models**

**Overview**

Combines multiple pricing strategies to maximize revenue while offering flexibility. Companies often blend subscription, usage-based, and success-based models.

Hybrid pricing suits AI platforms catering to a mix of individual users and enterprises, particularly in design, productivity, and automation. It’s ideal for AI solutions that benefit from both access fees and usage-based billing, such as AI-powered customer support or cloud-based AI processing tools. This model provides a balance between steady revenue and scalable pricing, making it an effective approach for AI-driven SaaS businesses.

**Real-Life Examples**

* **Salesforce Einstein AI:** Offers AI-driven CRM features as part of Salesforce’s subscription plans, with additional usage-based pricing for premium AI capabilities.
* **HubSpot:** Platform includes AI-powered tools in its subscription plans while offering additional premium AI features on a usage-based model.
* **Adobe Sensei AI:** Bundles AI-powered features within Adobe’s subscription plans but also includes usage-based pricing for certain cloud AI services.

**Pros & Cons**

Hybrid pricing maximizes revenue by combining fixed fees (subscription or per-user) with usage-based charges, capturing diverse customer segments. It stabilizes cash flow with predictable fees while allowing for scalable growth as customers increase usage. This model lowers adoption barriers for new users and ensures customers only pay for what they need, accommodating both small businesses and large enterprises. It also helps optimize monetization by charging high-volume users proportionally.

However, hybrid pricing can be complex, requiring clear communication to avoid confusion. It demands sophisticated billing systems for tracking multiple pricing components and can frustrate customers if not well-structured. Balancing fixed and variable costs is challenging, as overpricing fixed fees may limit adoption, while excessive reliance on usage-based charges creates revenue unpredictability.

---

####

### Closing Thoughts: Part 1

AI product pricing is evolving rapidly, and the models we’ve explored so far only scratch the surface. As businesses continue to integrate AI-driven pricing, understanding the full range of models, strategic approaches, and potential risks becomes essential for staying competitive. In **Part 2**, we’ll dive into five more key AI product pricing models, explore AI-driven pricing strategies, and examine the risks and ethical considerations that come with AI product pricing in today’s market.

# Retail pricing strategies: How AI keeps retailers profitable and popular

![](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Retail-pricing-strategies-Hero-image.png)

Years ago, when I was in the trenches in retail, I had an all-powerful, indestructible laptop to safeguard the massive spreadsheet I used to calculate and track pricing KPIs. The office lights dimmed when I powered up my computer.

So, I sympathize with today’s [category managers](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/category-management/) who are still mired in spreadsheets or outdated planning systems. Inaccuracies and inefficiencies are baked into the very processes they use to manage their retail pricing strategies. These processes can’t handle the requirements and scale of retail data analysis, no matter how heroically teams maintain their files.

The good news is that pricing solutions have advanced far beyond formulas and data tables teetering on the edge of breaking every time someone uses the office microwave.

AI-driven pricing solutions now integrate, process, and analyze data from across the organization in near real-time. This level of visibility and control gives retailers the rapid insights they need to effectively manage pricing decisions across locations, categories, and banners – every week.

Let’s explore how the right solution helps companies determine and execute optimal retail pricing strategies that protect margins, maintain a competitive price image, and increase customer satisfaction.

## Types of retail pricing strategies

A pricing strategy determines how a product will be priced to achieve a particular KPI. Before we talk about implementing a strategy, let’s consider our options.

There are five standard retail pricing strategies.

So now you have an array of strategies. How do you decide which strategy to apply to each product?

## The challenges of selecting a strategy and the dangers of getting it wrong

There’s a lot at stake when designing a retail strategy. A poor strategy can create inventory inefficiencies, erode margins, reduce your competitiveness, and damage customer trust.

You’ll have to wade through a lot of considerations and data before you can determine which pricing strategy best fits each product, category, location, and season.

![Six-panel diagram illustrating retail pricing challenges: massive datasets, price elasticity, price image, competition, disruption/complexity, and disjointed processes.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-1.png)

### Data, data everywhere, but we don’t know what to think

To determine the impact pricing decisions have on demand, retailers must gather enormous volumes of data from multiple sources and then store, process, and analyze that data to make effective decisions.

Plus, as a retailer, you may have 100 categories, multiplied by five banners, available in five different pricing regions. That’s a lot of input to analyze, often on a very tight schedule.

Most planning processes cannot handle and organize this flow of information at the rate the industry moves. Without a system that creates a cohesive picture of data at a granular, product-specific level, retailers won’t have the insights and visibility to plan efficiently, meet demand, and achieve margin targets.

### Determining price elasticity

Price elasticity refers to how drastically changes in price affect demand – a key consideration when devising a strategy.

Products that experience significant demand fluctuations in response to price changes are considered highly “elastic.” For example, if you put non-essential treats on sale – cookies, candy, chips – you’ll likely see a spike in demand as people stock up on their favorites.

Other products – usually staples like bread or milk – are “inelastic.” Demand for these products remains the same, regardless of price. If you raise the price of milk, consumers will likely still pay it because they consider milk a necessity. If you run a promotion, it probably won’t make a significant difference, especially for perishable items. People only go through so much milk in a week.

If you have the data processing capabilities to do it well, calculating price elasticity helps you determine how much flexibility you have when setting prices.

But there’s a catch. Strategies must be tempered by real-world factors like price image.

### Protecting your price image

“Price image” refers to the consumer’s perception of how much value a retailer provides.

So sure, you could raise prices on an inelastic item like milk and still see the same demand, but consumers will remember and begrudge your attempts to make your margins on these staple products.

They may also assume that if you’re raising prices on milk, your prices for non-essential items are too high. You may end up losing sales and customers in the long run – especially since [62%](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/how-retailers-can-thrive-in-price-sensitive-times/) of shoppers prioritize price and are willing to switch stores for better deals.

![Graph showing how consumers rated their favorite retailers. Roughly half reported their favorite retailers perform only "somewhat well" in offering competitive pricing, making their customers feel valued, and offering personalized shopping experiences.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/img-4-2.png)

In fact, 54% of shoppers report that their favorite retailer performs only “somewhat well” in offering competitive pricing, meaning many companies could lose customers to competitors that swoop in and offer better perceived value.

**Learn more:** [How retailers can thrive in price-sensitive times](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/how-retailers-can-thrive-in-price-sensitive-times/)

### Managing complexity

Aside from price elasticity and consumer loyalty, you also need to monitor your competition, market shifts, supply disruptions, geopolitical activities, and all the other factors impacting supply chain decisions.

For instance, is a national chain competitor opening down the street and running big promotions? Have market conditions upended the supply-to-demand ratio? Did a freak weather event delay shipments, forcing you to source from a more expensive supplier?

Amid this complexity, you have a careful balance to strike. For example, you may need several margin enhancers to offset the loss associated with every competitively priced item – while still maintaining a good price image and reacting to fluctuating circumstances.

You’ll need a cohesive, adaptable planning process to do so – and that’s another problem.

### Disconnected planning systems

Many retailers use pricing spreadsheets that are just not built to handle retail’s daily influx of information and rapid decision-making. Plus, they’re often not connected to any other planning functions within the company.

Let’s look at a hypothetical example.

A category manager, Jan, has set what she believes is a profitable pricing strategy based on the data available to her. Even if Jan has designed a great strategy in theory, it may not be feasible.

Let’s say she decides to competitively price laundry detergent, dropping the price by 10%.

![Diagram showing disconnected Pricing and Inventory planning systems. The pricing system shows a price drop that will increase sales and profit, but the inventory system shows empty shelves and boxes, indicating there isn’t enough inventory to support the spike in demand.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-3.png)

Does that price align with ongoing promotional campaigns? Is there enough inventory to support a sudden spike in demand? Do stores have enough shelf space to handle that extra inventory? The other planning teams may know, but without [connected planning modules](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/unified-pricing-and-promotion-planning-the-key-to-pricing-precision-and-powerful-promotions/), Jan doesn’t.

What’s more, if there is no link between Jan’s pricing solution and, say, the [inventory management solution](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/inventory-planning-software/), the inventory planners won’t know about Jan’s pricing changes and won’t realize the impact on demand until it’s too late. It’s a recipe for stock-outs and disgruntled customers.

![](https://fast.wistia.com/embed/medias/ur9dzhxokk/swatch)

This lack of supply chain collaboration and visibility leads to inefficiency and profit loss – not to mention frustration – across the business.

## The impact of AI on retail pricing strategies

Most retailers are almost [seven years](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/from-fragmented-to-unified-pricing-and-promotions-report-summ) behind advancements in pricing technology – and considering how rapidly AI is improving, this tech debt is growing exponentially. Many retailers are missing out on the near real-time data capabilities, advanced algorithms, and AI innovations that truly optimize pricing decisions.

These days, AI is a prerequisite for successful pricing strategies because it helps you understand how those strategies are performing and how to improve them. It analyzes huge swathes of data and weighs competing objectives and constraints so you can quickly gauge your best course of action.

An AI-driven tool like [RELEX Price Optimization](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/price-optimization-software/) helps you focus on and balance your top five KPIS:

Accounting for these goals, RELEX finds the best opportunities and items for price adjustments so teams can better understand how to generate more traffic, increase basket sizes, monitor competition, and protect price image.

## Five ways AI-driven pricing software enhances pricing strategies

So, how does RELEX help companies achieve all these benefits? What tools, methods, and technology do we use?

Our [pricing solution](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/what-is-price-planning-and-why-should-it-matter-to-retailers/) improves planning accuracy and speed by incorporating [different AI algorithms](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/ai-at-relex/) into an environment powerful enough to handle retail-scale data. It also applies consumer decision trees (CDTs) and scenario planning for even better-informed pricing decisions. But the benefits don’t stop there.

The solution integrates with the rest of the RELEX platform, connecting data and plans across functions and sparing teams the negative – and often unexpected – ripple effects of siloed decisions. With a unified platform, teams can plan efficiently and collaboratively, delivering shopping experiences that give customers good reason to keep coming back.

![Diagram showing five pricing solution requirements: Shared data, Fast AI-driven planning, CDT-informed decisions, End-to-end platform, and Scenario planning.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-4.png)

Let’s examine each of these capabilities in turn.

### 1. Sorting and analyzing data

Retail generates a lot of data, and the RELEX platform is built to handle it, capable of managing tens of millions of SKU-locations. The solution can pull information from across POS systems and different planning functions, while also incorporating external information concerning suppliers, weather, [seasonal events](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/markdown-pricing-and-optimization-software/), and more.

Whatever ERP, REST API, or bespoke process you’re using, RELEX channels these multiple data streams into a single data pool for easy collaboration. It also employs [data-cleansing capabilities](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/use-ai-to-make-messy-datasets-useful-not-perfect/) to ensure the information is accurate and up to date.

This [data-sharing foundation](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/does-your-database-deliver-how-specialization-pushes-the-boundaries-of-high-performance-data-processing/) feeds, streamlines, and improves all your AI calculations and supply chain decisions.

**Learn more:** [The IT Playbook for Successful Data Management](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/the-it-playbook-for-successful-data-management/)

### 2. Accelerating and improving planning with machine learning and gen AI

Once you have all that data, you need to process and analyze it quickly to keep up with the market, but planners have limited time and bandwidth. That’s where AI steps in. RELEX uses machine learning (ML) algorithms to eliminate the grunt work of forecast calculations and analyze data at superhuman speeds.

For instance, [ML-based forecasting](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/demand-planning-software/) can account for pricing and promotional initiatives to calculate how those teams’ decisions will affect overall demand.

![Four-panel illustration showing retail marketing elements: promotion types (boxes and coins), marketing activities (sale display), price reductions (cart with discounts), and in-store displays.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-5.png)

This combination of automation and planner expertise allows teams to focus on the exceptions that require a human judgement call, increasing the speed and accuracy of [forecasting decisions](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/measuring-forecast-accuracy/) and all the planning decisions that rely on those forecasts.

**Learn more**: [The complete guide to machine learning in retail demand forecasting](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/machine-learning-in-retail-demand-forecasting/)

The RELEX platform also gives planners access to Rebot, an interactive gen AI system that provides quick answers to planners’ industry and solution questions.

![A chat window between a RELEX user and Rebot. The user asks why an order proposal is so high. Rebot responds that planned promotions and an unexpected heat wave will raise demand for the item in question.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-6.png)

Plus, we’re actively developing Rebot into an assistant that will be able to provide company-specific recommendations and proactively assist planners in countering market disruptions.

### 3. Incorporating consumer decision trees

AI works with any data set you give it, but the more that data set reflects the real world, the better the outcomes.

RELEX uses [consumer decision trees (CDTs)](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/consumer-decision-trees/) to inject reality into AI calculations. The system learns from every consumer decision so teams can set better pricing rules.

For instance, let’s say the CDTs help you decipher consumer buying patterns that indicate negative customer responses to frequent or major price changes. You then set rules to prevent the system from changing prices more than once a month or by more than 10%. The system can also alert your teams if there is no price that meets every requirement so they can manage by exception.

With strategic rules guiding automation and improving decisions, your planners can focus on their targets. They have the insights they need to meet not only their primary objectives but also their secondary and tertiary targets, boosting overall business performance.

### 4. Running scenarios

Retail planning is full of “what-if” questions, and our pricing solution makes it possible for planners to answer them.

RELEX provides a digital twin, a replica of the physical supply chain, that allows teams to test outcomes based on real-world data.

For instance, say that a sudden disruption makes it likely that only 50% of an expected shipment will be delivered.

![Flowchart showing digital twin concept: production environment and digital twin models simulate disruption scenarios, comparing impacts on margins and service levels.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Img-2-Production-Planning.png)

Before committing to any specific action, planners can run scenarios in this sandbox environment to see which decision will most likely protect or balance their prioritized objectives.

**Learn more:** [Digital twins in supply chain management](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/digital-twin-supply-chain/)

### 5. Unifying planning processes to improve KPIs and reduce volatility

RELEX Price Optimization easily integrates with the rest of the [RELEX unified platform](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/scaling-ai-for-consumer-centric-growth/). Our platform helps you avoid fractured data and tunnel-vision planning by connecting every retail and supply chain planning function to a shared core of data and AI-driven calculations.

Let’s return to our category manager, Jan, for a moment to illustrate the impact of a unified planning solution on pricing decisions.

![Diagram showing connected Pricing and Inventory planning systems. The pricing system shows a price drop that increases sales and profit, and the inventory system shows boxes with a green check mark, indicating there is enough inventory to meet demand.](https://s32519.pcdn.co/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Fig-8.png)

This time, when Jan decides to competitively price laundry detergent, the unified solution can automatically check if there is a conflict with upcoming promotions.

The supply chain team also gets an alert and can verify if they can source enough stock at the right price to fulfill projected demand and make a profit.

At the [operations level](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/store-execution-software/), space planners can ensure there’s enough shelf space for the extra stock and position inventory where it will best attract customers.

This unified approach:

## Staying two steps ahead: Connecting retail pricing strategies to promotions

Retail pricing strategies are only as effective as the planning platform they’re built on. You can try to scale with spreadsheets until your laptop starts billowing smoke, but an AI-driven pricing tool will give you far better value for far longer, with the added bonus of not setting off your fire alarm.

But that’s not the end of the story. When it comes to implementing a new pricing solution, it pays to think two steps ahead.

Once that pricing solution is up and running, how do you ensure those strategies are effective and account for other planning decisions? What’s the best next step?

Many retailers plan to integrate their pricing module with their [promo planning](https://www.relexsolutions.com/solutions/promotion-planning-software/) solution. Promotions drove [$1 trillion](https://www.relexsolutions.com/resources/from-fragmented-to-unified-pricing-and-promotions-report-summary/) in sales in 2023, so it’s no surprise that [69%](https://hub.relexsolutions.com/pricing-promotions-relex-incisiv-research) of retailers reported interest in automating processes that communicate price changes across their organizations.

Connected AI-driven solutions are the foundation of investments that drive long-term ROI, and they hold the key to efficient and competitive price and promo planning.

A pricing strategy is the method retailers use to set prices to meet specific business goals while accounting for factors like cost, competition, demand, and customer behavior.

enriched_points.point_title: Increased use of AI and machine learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI and machine learning enhance pricing strategies by analyzing vast datasets, enabling dynamic price adjustments and optimizing profit margins based on real-time market conditions.
Personalized pricing strategies leverage customer data to tailor prices, improving customer satisfaction and increasing conversion rates as businesses align offers with individual preferences.

Blockchain technology ensures transparency in pricing by securely recording transactions, enhancing trust with customers through verifiable price histories and reducing the risk of price manipulation.
Ethical pricing practices prioritize fairness and transparency, fostering customer loyalty and brand reputation while complying with regulations to avoid exploitative pricing strategies.
Subscription-based models provide predictable revenue streams and customer retention by offering continuous access to products or services, catering to evolving consumer preferences for convenience.
